nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing, highly oxygenated rocky rivers and tributaries; crevices and caves in submerged rockwork over sandy or mixed substrate

decorPreferences: smooth rockwork with crevices and small caves
purpose-made ceramic or PVC breeding caves
driftwood for shelter and biofilm grazing
strong circulation (powerhead or spray bar) for high oxygenation

diet.notes: Carnivore-leaning omnivore. Staple diet of high-protein sinking foods: frozen bloodworm, white mosquito larvae, Mysis shrimp, chopped prawn, and carnivore/Loricariid pellets. Accepts vegetable matter when acclimated — algae wafers, blanched zucchini, cucumber, and spirulina-based foods. Unlike many plecos, it is not an algae grazer and should not be expected to clean algae from glass or décor. Feed after lights-out due to nocturnal feeding behaviour.

goodWith: apistogramma and small dwarf cichlids (with adequate caves)
avoidWith: other Hypancistrus or Loricariid plecos (cave competition and possible hybridisation with L340/L454/L474 variants)
boisterous or fast-feeding mid-level cichlids that outcompete at feeding time
any species intolerant of warm (26–30°C), fast-flowing, well-oxygenated water

summary: The Columbian Zebra Pleco (L129) is a small, striking Hypancistrus from Colombia’s Rio Bita in the lower Orinoco system. Reaching ~10 cm, it is carnivore-leaning, cave-dwelling, and captive-bred — a more accessible alternative to the export-banned Brazilian Zebra Pleco (H. zebra, L046). Intermediate care due to warm-water, high-flow demands.
careNotes:
Hypancistrus debilittera is collected primarily from the Rio Bita, a tributary of the Rio Meta within the Orinoco drainage on the Colombia–Venezuela border — making it the northernmost-ranging Hypancistrus in South America. It should not be confused with the true Zebra Pleco (H. zebra, L046) from the Rio Xingu in Brazil, which is subject to a Brazilian export ban and commands significantly higher prices; the L129 is widely captive-bred and comparably tractable for intermediate hobbyists.

Water quality and oxygenation are the most critical parameters: the species requires warm (23–30°C, ideally 26–28°C for maintenance), soft to moderately hard, slightly acidic to neutral water with strong flow and high dissolved oxygen. A powerhead or spray bar that directs current across cave entrances mimics the natural rocky riverine habitat. Robust mechanical and biological filtration is essential as these fish produce significant waste relative to their size. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are strongly recommended.

Despite being a pleco, L129 is not an algae grazer — do not rely on it to clean the aquarium. It is carnivore-leaning and thrives on protein-rich sinking foods offered after lights-out. Meaty frozen foods (bloodworm, Mysis shrimp, prawn pieces) should form the bulk of the diet, supplemented by quality sinking pellets formulated for Loricariids. Occasional vegetable matter (blanched zucchini or cucumber) is accepted and beneficial once the fish is settled.

Provide multiple caves (ceramic tubes, PVC pipes, or purpose-made Loricariid spawning caves) even for a single specimen, as this species is strongly cave-reliant for shelter and security. Males may quarrel over prime caves; a single male with two or three females in an appropriately sized tank with surplus caves generally reduces aggression. Keep only one Hypancistrus species per tank to prevent hybridisation with morphologically similar forms such as L340, L454, and L474.

Sexing: males develop longer odontodes (bristle-like spines) on the pectoral fin rays and cheeks, and have broader, flatter heads; females are rounder in the belly area. See also: [zebra-pleco] (H. zebra, L046) — the Brazilian congener; Brazilian export restrictions make wild-caught L046 extremely rare and expensive.
breedingNotes: Cave spawner; considered moderately difficult to breed. Condition adults on a varied high-protein diet and provide one small ceramic or PVC cave per fish (interior diameter just wide enough for the male to turn around). Raising temperature to 28–30°C and directing a powerhead current across cave entrances triggers spawning behaviour. The male guards the clutch of roughly 15–25 eggs inside the cave; eggs hatch in approximately 5–7 days. Fry remain in the cave consuming their yolk sac for a further week before free-swimming. First foods: newly hatched brine shrimp, micro-worm, and finely crushed carnivore pellet. Maintain excellent water quality throughout; fry are sensitive to ammonia spikes. A separate breeding or grow-out tank improves fry survival rates. Captive-bred stock is widely available in the hobby.

primary.name: AquaInfo — Hypancistrus debilittera Colombian Zebra Pleco L129
primary.notes: Primary husbandry reference. Provided adult size (7–8 cm TL), temperature (23–28°C, breeding 28–30°C), pH (5.5–7.5), GH (2–15 dH), native range (Rio Bita tributary of Rio Orinoco, upstream from Puerto Careño, Colombia/Venezuela), diet (primarily carnivorous; protein-rich staples plus vegetable matter when acclimated), tank dimensions (80×35 cm minimum; 100×40 cm for groups), breeding notes (cave spawner, 2–3 females per male ratio), and behaviour notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Hypancistrus debilittera
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), native range in Orinoco drainage (Venezuela/Colombia), IUCN Least Concern status, demersal freshwater habitat, and commercial aquarium trade status.

additional.name: Catfish Codex — Hypancistrus debilittera L129
additional.notes: Provided native range detail (Rio Bita and Rio Meta, Colombia; northernmost Hypancistrus distribution), temperature guidance (26–30°C), diet (crustaceans, insect larvae, fish meat plus quality dried food with vegetable content), adult size (up to 10 cm), breeding behaviour (male cave guarding, female injury risk), and hybridisation warning with L340/L454/L474.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping (Fishkeeper.co.uk) — Colombian Zebra Plec L129
additional.notes: Corroborated temperature (22–30°C), pH (5.5–7.2), hardness (up to 15 KH), native range (Venezuela and Colombia, Orinoco River basin), tank requirements (brisk water movement, oxygenation, sand substrate, rocky décor), diet (bloodworm, mosquito larvae, Mysis shrimp, chopped prawns, carnivore sinking pellets), and egg/breeding data (20–30 eggs, 5–7 day hatch).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Hypancistrus debilittera
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes, Ancistrin tribe within Hypostominae subfamily), described by Armbruster, Lujan & Taphorn 2007, native range (Venezuela and Colombia, Orinoco River system), IUCN Least Concern.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave spawner; male guards clutch of ~15–25 eggs in a tight cave; eggs hatch in 5–7 days. Trigger with 28–30°C water, strong directed flow across cave entrances, and protein-rich conditioning diet. Fry take newly hatched brine shrimp and micro-worm. Captive-bred stock readily available.
